paper.title: Comparison of Molly and Karoline models to predict methane production in growing and dairy cattle
paper.abstract_text: Numerous empirical and mechanistic models predicting methane (CH 4 ) production are available. The aim of this work was to evaluate the Molly cow model and the Nordic cow model Karoline in predicting CH 4  production in cattle using a data set consisting of 267 treatment means from 55 respiration chamber studies. The dietary and animal characteristics used for the model evaluation represent the range of diets fed to dairy and growing cattle. Feedlot diets and diets containing additives mitigating CH 4  production were not included in the data set. The relationships between observed and predicted CH 4  (pCH 4 ) were assessed by regression analysis using fixed and mixed model analysis. Residual analysis was conducted to evaluate which dietary factors were related to prediction errors. The fixed model analysis showed that the Molly predictions were related to the observed data (± standard error) as CH 4  (g/d) = 0.94 (±0.022) × pCH 4  (g/d) + 31 (±6.9) [root mean squared prediction error (RMSPE) = 45.0 g/d (14.9% of observed mean), concordance correlation coefficient (CCC) = 0.925]. The corresponding equation for the Karoline model was CH 4  (g/d) = CH 4  (g/d) = 0.98 (±0.019) × pCH 4  (g/d) + 7.0 (±6.0) [RMSPE = 35.0 g/d (11.6%), CCC = 0.953]. Proportions of mean squared prediction error attributable to mean and linear bias and random error were 10.6, 2.2, and 87.2% for the Molly model, and 1.3, 0.3, and 98.6% for the Karoline model, respectively. Mean and linear bias were significant for the Molly model but not for the Karoline model. With the mixed model regression analysis RMSPE adjusted for random study effects were 10.9 and 7.9% for the Molly model and the Karoline model, respectively. The residuals of CH 4  predictions were more strongly related to factors associated with CH 4  production (feeding level, digestibility, fat concentrations) with the Molly model compared with the Karoline model. Especially large mean (underprediction) and linear bias (overprediction of low digestibility diets relative to high digestibility diets) contributed to the prediction error of CH 4  yield with the Molly model. It was concluded that both models could be used for prediction of CH 4  production in cattle, but Karoline was more accurate and precise based on smaller RMSPE, mean bias, and slope bias, and greater CCC. The importance of accurate input data of key variables affecting diet digestibility is emphasized.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This equation defines RMSPE, the central goodness-of-fit criterion used in the paper to compare how well the Molly and Karoline models reproduce observed methane production and digestibility. It aggregates squared residuals between observed and predicted values across treatment means, allowing direct comparison of model accuracy on a common scale.
assumptions: Used to summarize prediction error for various response variables, notably methane production (g/d) and organic matter digestibility (g/kg), across 267 treatment means from 55 respiration chamber studies. Applies to both growing and dairy cattle and to predictions from the Molly and Karoline models.
Generic error metric; used for multiple output variables, not only methane.

source_spans.text_context: RMSPE = √[Σ (observed − predicted) 2 /n]. This equation defines the root mean squared prediction error used to quantify the discrepancy between observed and predicted values from the Molly and Karoline models.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This mixed-effects regression equation formalizes how the authors compare observed methane production or digestibility to predictions from the Molly and Karoline models. Fixed effects quantify overall bias and scaling, while random intercepts and slopes account for between-study differences, yielding more robust estimates of model performance across heterogeneous experiments.

parameters.name: random effect (slope for of X_{1ij}) of study

assumptions: Represents the mixed-effects regression structure used to relate observed responses (e.g., observed CH4 production) to predictors such as model-predicted CH4 (pCH4), accounting for random study effects in the 55 respiration chamber experiments.

The paper applies this structure with Y as observed CH4 or digestibility and X1ij typically as pCH4 or pOMD from each model; specific parameter estimates are reported in text or tables rather than in this generic form.

source_spans.text_context: [1] Y ij = B 0 + B 1 X 1ij + b 0 + b 1 X 1ij + e ij , presented in the Statistical Analysis section to describe the mixed model used to regress observed responses on predicted values with both fixed and random effects.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This regression quantifies the overall agreement between observed methane production and predictions from the Molly model. The slope near 1 and positive intercept indicate modest bias, while the relatively large RMSPE and notable mean and linear bias components highlight limitations of Molly when applied across the diverse data set.
assumptions: Fixed-effects regression relating observed CH4 production to Molly model predictions across 267 treatment means from 55 studies. Includes both growing and dairy cattle on non-feedlot diets without CH4-mitigating additives.
Represents the main fixed-effects evaluation of Molly for CH4; parameters are accompanied by standard errors in the text but only point estimates are recorded here.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: The fixed model analysis showed that the Molly predictions were related to the observed data (± standard error) as CH 4 (g/d) = 0.94 (SE ± 0.022) × pCH 4 (g/d) + 31 (±6.9) with RMSPE = 45.0 g/d (14.9% of observed mean), CCC = 0.925.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This fixed-effects regression measures how closely Karoline’s predicted methane production matches chamber observations. The slope very close to 1, small intercept, and lower RMSPE relative to Molly indicate that Karoline reproduces observed CH4 more accurately and with less systematic bias across the compiled data set.
assumptions: Fixed-effects regression relating observed CH4 production to Karoline model predictions over the same 267 treatment means from 55 respiration chamber studies covering growing and dairy cattle.
Key comparative equation showing Karoline’s superior fixed-effects performance for CH4 relative to Molly.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: The corresponding equation for the Karoline model was CH 4 (g/d) = 0.98 (±0.019) × pCH 4 (g/d) + 7.0 (±6.0) with RMSPE = 35.0 g/d (11.6% of observed mean), CCC = 0.953.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This equation provides a detailed regression of observed methane on Karoline-predicted values with mixed model analysis, associated with a lower RMSPE and percentage error compared to fixed model regression. It highlights Karoline’s strong predictive performance once between-study variability is modeled explicitly.
assumptions: Regression of observed CH4 on Karoline predictions reported in the context of model performance statistics (under mixed-effects analysis) for the full data set of 267 observations.

The text associates this equation with Karoline under mixed model analysis; the slightly lower slope (0.88) with higher intercept suggests some scale and offset bias even though overall error is relatively small.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: Karoline: CH 4 (g/d) = 0.88 (±0.027) × pCH 4 (g/d) + 42 (±9.7) (RMSPE = 24.0 g/d; 7.9% of observed mean).

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This regression represents another evaluation of Molly-predicted methane versus observed values, with mixed model analysis. Thex mixed model anaylsis resulted in better performance than fixed model anaylysis. But the combination of a slope notably less than 1, a relatively large positive intercept, and higher RMSPE compared with Karoline underscores Molly’s tendency toward systematic bias and lower precision.
assumptions: Regression of observed CH4 on Karoline predictions reported in the context of model performance statistics (under mixed-effects analysis) for the full data set of 267 observations.

The text associates this equation with Molly under mixed model analysis; reported RMSPE (33.1 g/d; 10.9% of observed mean) is larger than Karoline, indicating slightly worse performance in the prediction than Karoline's model.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: Molly: CH 4 (g/d) = 0.84 (±0.313) × pCH 4 (g/d) + 62 (±10.5) (RMSPE = 33.1 g/d; 10.9% of observed mean).

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This equation assesses how well Karoline predicts organic matter digestibility. The relatively high slope (0.79) and modest intercept (150), combined with very low RMSPE relative to the mean, indicate that Karoline captures digestibility patterns across diets with good fidelity, which is crucial because digestibility is a key driver of methane production.
assumptions: Relates observed organic matter digestibility to Karoline-predicted OMD for the same 267 diet treatments used in methane evaluation.
The formula text notes pOMD (g/d), but context indicates both OMD and pOMD should be in g/kg; that unit is adopted here, might be a typo in the original text.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: Karoline: OMD (g/kg) = 0.79(±0.045) × pOMD (g/d) + 150 (±33.4) (RMSPE = 19.7; 2.7% of observed mean).

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: This regression quantifies Molly’s ability to predict organic matter digestibility. The low slope and high intercept, together with a higher RMSPE than Karoline’s OMD regression, suggest systematic bias and weaker capture of digestibility, which likely contributes to Molly’s poorer methane predictions.
assumptions: Regression of observed OMD on Molly-predicted OMD across the same respiration chamber data set used for CH4 model evaluation.
The raw text includes a formatting anomaly (45.0.0); it is interpreted here as 45.0.

source_spans.section: RESULTS > Predictions of CH4 Production and Digestibility by the Karoline and Molly Models
source_spans.text_context: Molly: OMD (g/kg) = 0.39 (±0.064) × pOMD (g/kg) + 460 (±45.0.0) (RMSPE = 51.9; 7.0% of observed mean).
